Continuing the discussion from the other thread.

> The thing is that docstrings are only for python-exposed functions, and are not meant to serve as a documentation of the underlying C++.
> 
> The functions exposed to Python could return anything depending on the input parameters and documenting overloads or return types does not really make sense for sphinx.
> 
> I don't think that we should not try shoving a C++ mindset into the python-side documentation by adding return types.

My use case (and I imagine the use case of many others) is that I have a number of existing C++ libraries with fairly complete documentation in the header files. I want to be able to access that documentation directly from Python (or IPython, with the nifty "?" syntax). Writing the whole documentation *again* for Python would be unacceptable. Instead, I use a Python helper program (gendoc.py) that calls clang to extract the documentation automatically, which is nice because the Python docs are always up-to-date.

Because there are many overloaded functions (potentially with slightly different documentation text), it's key to have that information in the docstring, or using the library will be a confusing mess. The same goes for the return type -- when binding code from a C++ library, knowing the return type is implicitly a part of the documentation. In Python, the docstring may say so with English prose, but a C++ comment will often not have that explicitly written out because it's obvious from the function signature.

One potential workaround that I do see is that simple functions without overloads, the signature is propagaged to the first line, e.g.:
``
my_function(my_arg_1 : int = 1, my_arg_1 : bool = True)
``
For functions with multiple overloads, a generic (but truthful, that's what it really looks like internally) function prototype is added instead
``
my_function(*args, **kwargs)
``
The docstring could then continue with the short summary text followed by detailed signatures, e.g.:

```
1. Signature: my_function(my_arg_1 : int = 1, my_arg_1 : bool = True) -> NoneType

Documentation for overload 1

2. Signature: my_function(my_arg_1 : str) -> NoneType

Documentation for overload 2
```
This is just a suggestion, perhaps another variation will work better.

Hi @ppKrauss 

This is a pretty common source of confusion that's surprising at first but makes sense after some explanation about what the Let's Encrypt considers a renewal to be:

Your Certbot issued certificate covers the domains:
> oficial.news,api.ok.org.br, baixar.oficial.news, baixar.ok.org.br,.....

The expiration warning was for a certificate that covers the domains:
> baixar.ok.org.br, datasets.ok.org.br

The Let's Encrypt [documentation for expiration warning emails](https://letsencrypt.org/docs/expiration-emails/) explains why you received this warning message:
> We consider a certificate to be renewed if there is a newer certificate with the exact same set of names, regardless of which account created it. If you’ve issued a new certificate that adds or removes a name relative to your old certificate, you will get expiration email about your old certificate.

This warning was for a different certificate. Your new certificate is for a different set of names and so it isn't a renewal of the old certificate. 

If you aren't using the old certificate you can safely ignore the email.

I hope that makes sense. I'm going to close this issue since it isn't a bug that can be fixed by the Certbot team (the expiration emails come from [Boulder](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder), the Let's Encrypt server-side CA).

This doesn't prevent certain users from changing the defaults for their application, but the majority of "dumb" nodes should already behave as if they were under constant attack.

Nice! It would probably be good to clarify all of these points in the implementation details. 

For context, our [original implementation of Hyperband](https://github.com/richardliaw/ray/blob/f945ef479fe13dc9012cfe4511cfd0efda7686c0/python/ray/tune/hyperband.py) actually followed the paper, but after using it for a while, we realized that the given parameters led to many unintuitive and impractical bracket configurations. 

1. Both `s_max` (representing the number of brackets - 1) and `eta`, representing the downsampling rate, are fixed.  In many practical settings, you may have `R >= 200`. For simplicity, assume `eta = 3`. Between `R = 200` and `R = 1000`, you end up with a huge range of the number trials needed to fill up all brackets - 
![screenshot 2018-02-27 11 33 38](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4529381/36750419-4f3ebc9e-1bb2-11e8-9d0b-1f6c491efdfd.png)
Now, assume `R = 300`. If you want to find a better `eta`, you realize also that the configurations generated aren't very intuitive:
![screenshot 2018-02-27 11 36 55](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4529381/36750529-9baf7a32-1bb2-11e8-856f-772bbcd67ac9.png)
We figured that it would be a lot easier for majority of people to use if we fixed the parameters to be the same as the example given in the paper and only exposed `max_t`. I actually wouldn't worry too much about these deviations from theory, because HyperBand is actually quite inefficient in implementation (both in our experience and [noted by the authors](https://openreview.net/forum?id=S1Y7OOlRZ), who propose a fix that [we will soon provide](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/1595)).

2. The example in the post to calculate `n_0` is actually a little different than the algorithm given in the paper. 
Screenshot from the original paper: 
![screenshot 2018-02-27 10 55 09](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4529381/36751005-c7bb4664-1bb3-11e8-87b5-f76dab8237b3.png)

In fact, not rounding the calculation inside actually provides fairer resource allocation to all of the brackets.
![screenshot 2018-02-27 11 54 21](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4529381/36751413-f9c2a5b6-1bb4-11e8-83d5-bafda8e6aa1d.png)

3. There are also implementation specific details like how trials are placed into brackets which are not covered in the paper. Also, the recent PR #1620 aims to cut trials after `max_t`, which might actually not be the semantic definition in the paper, but makes much more sense to the end user.

tldr implementation details may deviate slightly from theory but are focused on increasing usability
Note that this is not a satisfactory solution, but it is better than a segfault.

The current problem with 

```python
    return redirect(request.args.get('The') if 'hey' or 'you' else request.args.get('French') if 'foo' else request.args.get('Aces') and 'c')
```

is that `expr_star_handler` return the tainted `aces` call as a last expression, because `StrNode`s never get added to `cfg_expressions`,  `get_last_expressions` can't see that `aces` is not the last expression. Hmmm, I fixed it with this:

```python
--- a/pyt/cfg/expr_visitor.py
+++ b/pyt/cfg/expr_visitor.py
@@ -86,6 +86,7 @@ class ExprVisitor(StmtVisitor):
         variables = list()
         visual_variables = list()
         first_node = None
+        last_node_should_not_be_ignored = False
         node_not_to_step_past = self.nodes[-1]
 
         for expr in exprs:
@@ -112,7 +113,8 @@ class ExprVisitor(StmtVisitor):
                 label.visit(expr)
                 visual_variables.append(label.result)
 
-            if not isinstance(node, StrNode):
+            last_node_should_not_be_ignored = not isinstance(node, StrNode)
+            if last_node_should_not_be_ignored:
                 cfg_expressions.append(node)
                 if not first_node:
                     if isinstance(node, ControlFlowExpr):
@@ -130,7 +132,7 @@ class ExprVisitor(StmtVisitor):
         return ConnectExpressions(
             first_expression=first_node,
             all_expressions=cfg_expressions,
-            last_expressions=get_last_expressions(cfg_expressions) if cfg_expressions else [],
+            last_expressions=get_last_expressions(cfg_expressions) if last_node_should_not_be_ignored else [],
             variables=variables,
             visual_variables=visual_variables
         )
```


Now it only reports a vuln, if `aces` was the last expression!

I agree that the list API is not very intuitive and the results of several API attempt iterations (no blame for that).

Most new python library always return iterator. if you really want a list, then you just call list()
e.g. in py3 range returns iterator, if you want old behaviour, you just do:

```l = list(range(10))```

If I were to redesign the API to be more pythonic, maybe we could just add an iter() method, and even an __iter__ method()

- list() always return a list (has all=False by default, and as_list=True)
- iter() always return a generator (and has paging and filtering arguments for those who cares)
- __iter__() is a shortcut for iter()
